related_content.title: Related Content
points: Apollo - Greek god of prophecy at Delphi
Odin - Norse seeker of prophetic wisdom
Morrigan - Celtic war goddess and prophetess
Celestial Beings - Messengers of divine will

keyMyths.title: The Oracle of Delphi and Oedipus
keyMyths.description: Oedipus's fate is sealed by a prophecy from the Oracle of Delphi, foretelling that he will kill his father and marry his mother. Despite attempts to avoid this fate, Oedipus unknowingly fulfills the prophecy, highlighting the power and inevitability of prophetic pronouncements.

keyMyths.title: Cassandra's Unheeded Warnings
keyMyths.description: Cassandra, daughter of Priam, is cursed by Apollo with the gift of prophecy, but also with the curse that no one will ever believe her. She foresees the fall of Troy but is powerless to prevent it, illustrating the tragic consequences of disbelief and the frustration of knowing the future without the ability to change it.

keyMyths.title: The Prophecy of Ragnarok
keyMyths.description: In Norse mythology, Ragnarok is a cataclysmic event foretold in various prophecies, detailing the end of the world and the battle between gods and giants. The prophecies describe specific events and the roles of various deities, emphasizing the cyclical nature of destruction and rebirth.

keyMyths.title: The Three Fates
keyMyths.description: The Three Fates (Moirai in Greek, Parcae in Roman) control the thread of life for every mortal and immortal, spinning, measuring, and cutting the thread to determine destiny. Their pronouncements are unchangeable, representing the inflexible nature of fate and the predetermined course of events.

keyMyths.title: The Sibylline Oracles
keyMyths.description: The Sibylline Oracles are a collection of prophecies written in Greek hexameters, attributed to various sibyls throughout antiquity. These oracles were consulted on matters of state and personal destiny, reflecting the belief in divine guidance and the importance of interpreting prophetic signs.
